comments.text: That sequence should work indeed. Heuristically reasons: $q_n+q_i-1$ is odd, and residues are not fixed modulo an odd prime. The probability that a number of the order $x$ is prime is roughly $1/ \log x$. Thus on average $q_{n+1}=q_n+\log(q_n)^{2+o(1)}$ and the expectation of failing should be small (we estimate $q_n=n^{1+o(1)}$ and probability of failing $(1-1/\log q_n)^n=exp(-n^{1-o(1)})$. An easy check shows that the list continues at least a million steps, and $\sum_{n=10^6}^{\infty} \exp(-n^{1-o(1)})$ will be very small.
